title: setting UX page has a lot of white space on top
body:
## 🐛 Bug Report

setting UX page has a lot of white space on top.

---

**Category:** `bug`
**Source:** Telegram Bot
**Status:** Pending implementation

> _This issue was created automatically via the BabyBloom Telegram Bot. No private baby data is included._
enhanced_description:
The Settings page has excessive white/blank space at the top, between the header and the first content section.

**Root cause:** The header uses the `.app-header` CSS class which has `position: fixed` by default (with safe-area-inset padding), but the component overrides it to `position: sticky`. The content area below starts at `padding: 16px` which may not account for the header height correctly, or the safe-area-inset-top adds large padding that creates visible white space on certain devices.

**Component affected:** `src/features/settings/Settings.tsx` — the header div (lines 89-117) and content wrapper (line 119).

**Expected behaviour:** The content should start immediately below the header with minimal spacing — no large blank gap.

**Current behaviour:** A significant white/blank area appears between the top of the page and the first visible content (the privacy notice banner).

**Edge cases:**
- Devices with notch/dynamic island may have larger safe-area-inset-top
- Test on both notched and non-notched devices/viewports

**Acceptance criteria:**
- No excessive white space visible between the header and the privacy notice
- Header still properly accounts for safe-area-inset on notched devices
- Page scrolling behaviour remains smooth

**Testing:** Add unit test verifying the Settings header renders without excessive top margin. Add visual regression test for the Settings page layout.
implementation_notes:
Fixed excessive white space on Settings page header.

- Files changed:
  - src/features/settings/Settings.tsx: Removed app-header CSS class from Settings header. The app-header class applies position:fixed with large safe-area-inset padding that created a visible white gap when overridden to position:sticky. Replaced with inline styles that directly control padding with a minimal safe-area-inset-top calculation.
- Approach: The header now uses its own flex layout with controlled padding (12px + safe-area-inset-top) instead of inheriting the global app-header class meant for the fixed main header.
- Trade-offs: Settings header no longer shares styles with the main app header, but this is correct since they serve different purposes (sticky vs fixed).

title: setting UX page as an icon before “Settings” which is white
body:
## 🐛 Bug Report

setting UX page as an icon before “Settings” which is white

---

**Category:** `bug`
**Source:** Telegram Bot
**Status:** Pending implementation

> _This issue was created automatically via the BabyBloom Telegram Bot. No private baby data is included._
enhanced_description:
The Settings page header has a back-arrow icon (arrow-left) before the "Settings" title text. This icon appears white or very light, making it invisible or hard to see against the header background.

**Component affected:** `src/features/settings/Settings.tsx` — the header back button (lines 100-115) uses `<Icon n="arrow-left" s={18} c={C.t} />` where `C.t` is the text color from the color constants.

**Expected behaviour:** The back-arrow icon should be clearly visible with good contrast against the header background (`C.bg`).

**Current behaviour:** The icon color (`C.t`) appears white or too light, blending into the background and being hard to see or invisible.

**Possible fix:** Ensure the icon color has sufficient contrast against the header background. Check if `C.t` resolves to a very light color. Consider using a darker/more contrasting color, or add a visible background container around the icon.

**Edge cases:**
- Dark mode vs light mode if applicable
- Different screen brightness settings

**Acceptance criteria:**
- The back-arrow icon is clearly visible with good contrast
- The icon remains visually consistent with the app's design language
- Icon is accessible (meets WCAG contrast ratios)

**Testing:** Add unit test verifying the Settings header back button renders with the correct icon color. Add test that the Icon component receives a color prop with sufficient contrast.
implementation_notes:
Fixed invisible back-arrow icon in Settings header.

- Files changed:
  - src/lib/constants/icons.ts: Added missing 'arrow-left' SVG path to the icon constants. The Icon component renders empty SVGs for unknown icon names, so the missing path made the back button invisible.
  - src/features/settings/Settings.tsx: Added a visible settings gear icon (using C.p primary pink color) next to the 'Settings' title text for better header identity.
- Root cause: The arrow-left icon was being used in Settings.tsx but its SVG path was never defined in icons.ts, resulting in an empty/invisible SVG.
- Also added: bell, mic, palette, database, info icon paths for the section header redesign.

title: Settings page is not professional and intuitive
body:
## 🐛 Bug Report

Settings page is not professional and intuitive.

---

**Category:** `bug`
**Source:** Telegram Bot
**Status:** Pending implementation

> _This issue was created automatically via the BabyBloom Telegram Bot. No private baby data is included._
enhanced_description:
The Settings page overall layout and UX does not feel professional or intuitive. This is a general UX improvement request covering the entire Settings page.

**Component affected:** `src/features/settings/Settings.tsx` — the entire page layout and section organization.

**Current issues observed:**
- Excessive white space at the top (related to #134)
- Icon visibility issues in the header (related to #135)
- Many sections stacked vertically with no visual hierarchy or grouping
- Section cards all look the same — no visual differentiation between important settings (profiles, country) vs secondary ones (guide, data, reset)
- The page has 9 sections which may feel overwhelming: Profiles, Country, General, Hero Background, Feeding Reminders, Shortcuts, Guide, Data, Reset
- No search or quick-navigation for settings
- Emoji-based section icons (emoji before section titles) may look informal/unprofessional

**Expected behaviour:** The Settings page should feel polished, well-organized, and easy to navigate. Important settings should be visually prominent, and the page should have clear visual hierarchy.

**Suggested improvements:**
- Group related settings (e.g., "Personalization" group for Country + Volume + Hero Background)
- Add visual hierarchy — primary settings more prominent than secondary
- Consider collapsible sections or a grouped card approach
- Use consistent, professional iconography instead of emojis for section headers
- Reduce total visible sections by grouping related items
- Ensure proper spacing and padding throughout

**Edge cases:**
- Settings page with no profiles (new user)
- Small screen devices — ensure sections don't overflow
- Long country lists

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Settings page feels organized and professional
- Clear visual hierarchy between primary and secondary settings
- Sections are logically grouped
- All interactive elements are easy to find and use
- No regression in existing functionality

**Testing:** Add unit tests for the reorganized Settings component. Test that all settings sections render correctly. Test that grouping/collapsing works if implemented. Verify no functional regression in settings changes (birth date, volume unit, country, reminders, etc.).
implementation_notes:
Redesigned Settings page for professional, intuitive UX.

- Files changed:
  - src/features/settings/Settings.tsx: Complete Section component redesign.
    - Replaced emoji-based section icons with Icon components using iconName/iconColor props
    - Added GroupLabel component for visual hierarchy: 'Profile & Region', 'Preferences', 'Data & Support'
    - Reduced spacing and padding for a tighter, more professional layout
    - Each section now has a color-coded icon matching its purpose (users=pink, bell=warning, database=accent, etc.)
  - src/lib/constants/icons.ts: Added 6 new icons (arrow-left, bell, mic, palette, database, info) to support icon-based section headers
  - tests/unit/settings-ui.test.ts: New test file covering all three issues (header layout, icon visibility, professional design)
- Approach: Maintained all existing functionality while improving visual hierarchy through grouped sections and consistent iconography.
- Trade-offs: Section component signature changed from icon (emoji string) to iconName/iconColor (Icon component props). Country flags still use emoji since they are actual flag characters, not design icons.

title: same notification comes multiple times- example if feed is due everytime I op...
body:
## 🐛 Bug Report

same notification comes multiple times- example if feed is due everytime I open the app it shows as notification

---

**Category:** `bug` `feeding`
**Source:** Telegram Bot
**Status:** Pending implementation

> _This issue was created automatically via the BabyBloom Telegram Bot. No private baby data is included._
enhanced_description:
Bug: Duplicate feed notifications on every app open.

- What the user reported: When a feed is due, the app shows a notification every time the user opens the app, rather than showing it once and then dismissing or suppressing it until the next scheduled feed.
- What component/area is affected: Notification system, specifically feed-related reminders/alerts. Likely involves the notification scheduling or display logic in the feed tracking components.
- Expected behaviour: A feed-due notification should appear once. After the user has seen or dismissed it, it should not reappear on subsequent app opens until a new feed event is actually due.
- Current behaviour: The same feed-due notification fires repeatedly every time the app is opened, creating a spammy experience.
- Edge cases to handle:
  - If the user opens the app but does not dismiss the notification, it may be acceptable to show it again — but not indefinitely.
  - Multiple feeds overdue simultaneously should each notify once, not multiply.
  - Ensure notifications still fire correctly after the fix (no regressions where notifications stop entirely).
  - Consider the case where the app is force-closed and reopened — the notification should still show once if not yet acknowledged.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Feed-due notifications appear only once per feed event.
  - Reopening the app does not re-trigger an already-shown notification.
  - Logging a feed clears any pending notification for that feed.
  - No regression in notification delivery for genuinely new feed events.
- Testing: Add unit tests for the notification deduplication logic — verify that a notification marked as shown is not re-triggered on app open. Add a regression test that simulates opening the app multiple times with an overdue feed and asserts the notification fires only once.
